geopolitical.market_impact: Energy costs are the dominant transmission mechanism: WTI at $88.48 directly raises datacenter operating costs, threatening AI capex ROI and pressuring semis. Defense names benefit, but the broad tech complex is caught between robust AI demand and energy inflation. Singapore banks face SME loan-quality risks from Middle East turmoil.
geopolitical.new_since_yesterday: Houthi militia announced a naval blockade of Saudi Arabia and threatened vessels carrying Saudi oil through Bab el-Mandeb, causing three Saudi tankers to reverse course. This adds a second chokepoint to the existing Hormuz crisis.

base.description: Hormuz crisis persists at current intensity with WTI in the $85-95 range; the Fed holds rates on July 29. Semis remain capped below their 50-day averages as the earnings gantlet unfolds, with MU and TSM holding relative strength.

optimistic.description: A ceasefire or de-escalation in the Strait of Hormuz and Bab el-Mandeb brings WTI back toward $75-80, removing the energy inflation overhang. Semis rally on AI demand conviction, with NVDA and ASML leading a re-rating.

pessimistic.description: Full closure of both Hormuz and Bab el-Mandeb sends WTI above $100, triggering a Fed rate hike on July 29. Tech sells off sharply, with the SOXX index dropping below key support; only HBM names like MU and SK Hynix hold relative strength.

wildcard.description: A sudden US-Iran diplomatic breakthrough or a Chinese AI model breakthrough (e.g., Kimi K3) reshapes the AI landscape, causing a violent rotation out of US hardware into Chinese tech or a collapse in the energy premium.

geopolitical.portfolio_action: No new money deployed until the July 29 FOMC passes and the Big Tech earnings gantlet provides clarity on capex trajectories. Cash is currently the highest-probability position.
